ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for brinjal pachadi as a prepared South Indian dish; mapped to eggplant food product as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked aubergine/eggplant; brinjal pachadi is a spiced, tamarind-sweetened preparation not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for brinjal pachadi as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of brinjal pachadi as a side dish is approximately 100g, served alongside rice or flatbread as part of a South Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Brinjal Pachadi — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Brinjal Pachadi (cr_id F14080005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this exact entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable dish with tamarind and sugar
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brinjal pachadi. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous mixed vegetable dish with added sugar and tamarind from the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 falls in the low category. The moderate fibre content (3.0g/100g) and tamarind acidity support a low-to-medium boundary estimate; 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney scale. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (55 × 24.9) / 100 = 13.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 905mg/100g — far exceeds the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Brinjal pachadi is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to extremely high sodium content (905mg/100g); preparation should significantly reduce added salt, and portion size should be strictly limited if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 16.5g/100g exceeds the 15g/100g diabetes threshold; moderate fibre (3.0g/100g) provides insufficient buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brinjal pachadi is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to high added sugar (16.5g/100g) driving rapid glycaemic response; unsweetened versions with reduced tamarind sugar are recommended, and portion size should be minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 905mg/100g and estimated saturated fat 6.5g/100g from coconut oil both elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brinjal pachadi poses high cardiovascular risk due to very high sodium (905mg/100g) promoting hypertension and estimated saturated fat (~6.5g/100g from coconut oil) contributing to LDL elevation; replacing coconut oil with olive oil and drastically reducing salt are recommended modifications.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Brinjal is a nightshade (solanine); sodium 905mg/100g exceeds 400mg/100g RA threshold; estimated saturated fat ~6.5g/100g above 5g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid brinjal pachadi due to nightshade solanine content, very high sodium promoting tissue swelling, and high saturated fat from coconut oil triggering pro-inflammatory cytokines; monitor personal nightshade sensitivity carefully.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brinjal pachadi is a South Indian vegetable side dish made from stir-fried brinjal (eggplant/aubergine) cooked with thick tamarind juice, added sugar, coconut oil, mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chilli, and turmeric. It is a popular accompaniment in South Indian and Singaporean Indian cuisine, typically served alongside steamed rice or flatbreads such as chapati. The dish has a complex flavour profile combining the earthy bitterness of brinjal with the sharp sourness of tamarind, sweetness from added sugar, and aromatic heat from chilli and curry leaves. The texture is soft and slightly sticky from the reduced tamarind-sugar glaze. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 179 kcal, 1.1g protein, 8.4g fat (predominantly saturated from coconut oil, estimated ~6.5g), 24.9g carbohydrate, 16.5g sugar, 3.0g dietary fibre, and 905mg sodium. The very high sodium content (905mg/100g) and high sugar content (16.5g/100g) make this dish clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant recipe modification. Brinjal is a nightshade vegetable and may trigger joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis-sensitive individuals. The turmeric content provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties, though this benefit is insufficient to offset the sodium and nightshade concerns for RA patients.
common_user_queries: Is brinjal pachadi high in sodium?
What does brinjal pachadi taste like?
Is eggplant pachadi suitable for hypertension?
Is pachadi good for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080005) with confidence 0.95; exact match to the named dish. Saturated fat estimated at ~6.5g/100g based on coconut oil content typical of this preparation, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for brinjal pachadi are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080005 as of 2026. saturated_fat_g estimated at 6.5g/100g based on typical coconut oil contribution in South Indian pachadi preparations; this is an approximation and not directly reported by HPB. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable dish with added sugar from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for brinjal pachadi exists in approved sources. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as low; accordingly gi_category is set to 'low'.

Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification rationale explicitly.
